FileNotFoundError does not exist in Python 2. I think IOError for Python 2 and FileNotFoundError for Python 3 makes the most sense. FileNotFoundError only exists in version Python 3.3 or later but that is fine since we are not targeting earlier Python 3 versions.

```python

@ureg.wraps(ureg.inch, ())
def f():
    return 39.97
```

Hi kiddick, bind and unbind are specially managed by the REUSABLE strategy. They are not performed untill needed. If you want to close all the underlying RESTARTABLE connection you must use the terminate_pool() method of the ConnectionPool. It will unbind all the connections and join all the threads back to the main thread.

Also, the connections are automatically unbound when the number of seconds specified in the ``pool_lifetime`` parameter of the Connection object.

source: https://developers.google.com/kml/documentation/kml_tut

Ground overlays enable you to "drape" an image onto the Earth's terrain. The <Icon> element contains the link to the .jpg file with the overlay image. Here is the example ground overlay in the KML Samples file, which shows Mount Etna erupting in 2001:
```
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<kml xmlns="http://www.opengis.net/kml/2.2">
  <Folder>
    <name>Ground Overlays</name>
    <description>Examples of ground overlays</description>
    <GroundOverlay>
      <name>Large-scale overlay on terrain</name>
      <description>Overlay shows Mount Etna erupting 
          on July 13th, 2001.</description>
      <Icon>
        <href>http://developers.google.com/kml/documentation/images/etna.jpg</href>
      </Icon>
      <LatLonBox>
        <north>37.91904192681665</north>
        <south>37.46543388598137</south>
        <east>15.35832653742206</east>
        <west>14.60128369746704</west>
        <rotation>-0.1556640799496235</rotation>
      </LatLonBox>
    </GroundOverlay>
  </Folder>
</kml>
```
While I'm here.... 

Many different types of paths can be created in Google Earth, and it is easy to be very creative with your data. In KML, a path is created by a <LineString> element. Take a look at the "Absolute Extruded" example in the Paths folder and you can see how the shape has been generated by the following code:

Notice how it is really just one line drawn at altitude above the ground. The <tessellate> tag breaks the line up into smaller chunks, and the <extrude> tag extends the line down to the ground.

```
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<kml xmlns="http://www.opengis.net/kml/2.2">
  <Document>
    <name>Paths</name>
    <description>Examples of paths. Note that the tessellate tag is by default
      set to 0. If you want to create tessellated lines, they must be authored
      (or edited) directly in KML.</description>
    <Style id="yellowLineGreenPoly">
      <LineStyle>
        <color>7f00ffff</color>
        <width>4</width>
      </LineStyle>
      <PolyStyle>
        <color>7f00ff00</color>
      </PolyStyle>
    </Style>
    <Placemark>
      <name>Absolute Extruded</name>
      <description>Transparent green wall with yellow outlines</description>
      <styleUrl>#yellowLineGreenPoly</styleUrl>
      <LineString>
        <extrude>1</extrude>
        <tessellate>1</tessellate>
        <altitudeMode>absolute</altitudeMode>
        <coordinates> -112.2550785337791,36.07954952145647,2357
          -112.2549277039738,36.08117083492122,2357
          -112.2552505069063,36.08260761307279,2357
          -112.2564540158376,36.08395660588506,2357
          -112.2580238976449,36.08511401044813,2357
          -112.2595218489022,36.08584355239394,2357
          -112.2608216347552,36.08612634548589,2357
          -112.262073428656,36.08626019085147,2357
          -112.2633204928495,36.08621519860091,2357
          -112.2644963846444,36.08627897945274,2357
          -112.2656969554589,36.08649599090644,2357 
        </coordinates>
      </LineString>
    </Placemark>
  </Document>
```

I actually included (in https://github.com/mikedh/trimesh/commit/0bd3b4341714cce73685b51e9303a18103c85982) the bitbanging logic in `hashable_rows` after an automatic precision check which gets the speedup into other grouping functions too (including unique_rows). 


Actually getting a version of `is_watertight` that didn't break things elsewhere in trimesh was a bit tougher than I'd expected, because so many things rely on it. This version of `trimesh.graph.is_watertight` does pass all tests, but is 50% slower than the original:

```
def is_watertight(edges, edges_sorted=None):

    # passing edges_sorted is a speedup only                                                 
    if edges_sorted is None:                                                                 
        edges_sorted = np.sort(edges, axis=1)                                                
                                                                                             
    # find the unique edges and the inverse to reconstruct                                   
    unique, inverse = grouping.unique_rows(edges_sorted)                                     
                                                                                             
    # if every edge is shared by an even number of faces                                     
    watertight = (np.mod(np.bincount(inverse), 2) == 0).all()                                
                                                                                             
    if watertight:                                                                           
        # a check to see if every shared edge is reversed                                    
        # note that this is robust for edges shared by two faces                             
        # but is subject to random ordering for edges shared by 4+ faces                     
        # and will return arbitrary results                                                  
        ordered = edges[inverse.argsort()]                                                   
        winding = not bool(np.equal(ordered[0::2],                                           
                                    ordered[1::2]).all(axis=1).any())                        
    else:                                                                                    
        winding = False                                                                      
                                                                                             
    return bool(watertight), bool(winding)              
```

I'm kind of on the fence about pushing it to master as it is slower, and it kind of jankily handles the winding check on multiple shared edges.

I've been giving it some more thought, and the only situation that I can come up with that would cause non-timeout login errors to fail initially, then succeed on subsequent retry is if bits were flipped in transit without triggering CRC / checksum failures. That failure mode to my knowledge is pretty uncommon, and should probably be ignored by network software (i.e. imagine if `requests` retried on `HTTP 400` just-in-case that 400 was really a 200 as sent by the server)

That said, regardless of whether the connection is made over telnet or SSH, if it's a very slow / unreliable / error-prone link, exceptions are going to occur sometimes anyway. If the user really needs a particular connection to occur if at all possible, then they'll want to perform exception handling and retries in their own code in either case in my opinion.

# client.py
```
from __future__ import print_function

from twisted.internet.task import react
from twisted.internet.defer import inlineCallbacks
from twisted.internet.endpoints import TCP4ClientEndpoint
from twisted.python.filepath import FilePath
from twisted.internet import ssl, task, protocol, endpoints, defer
from twisted.python.modules import getModule
from txsocksx.tls import TLSWrapClientEndpoint
from txsocksx.client import SOCKS5ClientEndpoint
import txtorcon
import other_proto


@defer.inlineCallbacks
def main(reactor):
    factory = protocol.Factory.forProtocol(other_proto.DecisionProtocol)
    ca_data = FilePath(b'ca_cert.pem').getContent()
    client_data = FilePath(b'a.client.pem').getContent()
    ca_cert = ssl.Certificate.loadPEM(ca_data)
    client_key = ssl.PrivateCertificate.loadPEM(client_data)
    options = ssl.optionsForClientTLS(u'the-authority', ca_cert, client_key)
    exampleEndpoint = txtorcon.TorClientEndpoint(ip, 8966, socks_hostname="127.0.0.1")
    tlsEndpoint = TLSWrapClientEndpoint(options, exampleEndpoint)
    deferred = yield tlsEndpoint.connect(factory)
    done = defer.Deferred()
    deferred.connectionLost = lambda reason: done.callback(None)
    yield done

task.react(main)  # (1)
```

You're right though. You can't use udp.srcport as a decode option. tshark doesn't accept it and that's why the loop never runs.

If what you mean is that you want both access to the Packet Summary class and the packet data fields, that can not be done with a single class at the moment (since tshark only allows one at a time).

You can, however, do this:

```python
packets = pyshark.FileCapture('/path/to/cap')
summaries = pyshark.FileCapture('/path/to/cap', only_summaries=True)

for packet, summary in zip(packets, summaries):
    print summary
```

"Zip" simply takes on item from each iterator.

So, the default limit of iterations is 100, which is usually sufficient for interactive work to produce something reasonable and not wait too long, but for difficult plots it might help to increase it. Also the function actually outputs the number of iterations is used, so if you want to see whether this is the problem, you can just print it out... Also for "perfect" plots you can set `precision=0`, although the default threshold is very small anyway.

> Your comment at the bottom of that readme influenced me: I wanted to avoid using a rotation matrix.

I'm not sure I understand what you're talking about.  The only comment I see is about Euler angles.  Just to be clear, Euler angles should absolutely never be used — ever.  Period.  Never.

Rotation matrices are completely separate things.  They should *usually* be avoided, because multiplying matrices is much less efficient than multiplying quaternions, and after a few multiplications the errors will multiply, giving you "rotation" matrices that aren't really rotations.  But if you're starting with a quaternion and converting to a matrix, there's not a lot of room for error to creep in, so they're fine.  The one time when you actually *should* use a matrix is as in your case, where you want to rotate multiple vectors.

Also, in case you missed it, I'll also point out the [new function `quaternion.rotate_vectors`](https://github.com/moble/quaternion/blob/master/__init__.py#L427), which does the rotations and handles the various possible dimensions nicely, with no need to copy or reshape data.  So your code could look as simple as

```python
import numpy as np
import quaternion

xyz_ary = np.random.rand(100,3)
qrot = np.exp(quaternion.quaternion(1, 0, 0) * 45 * (np.pi/180) / 2)
rotd_xyz_ary = quaternion.rotate_vectors(qrot, xyz_ary)
```

    padded_data = padder.update(data) + padder.finalize()

    cipher = Cipher(algorithms.AES(key), modes.CBC(iv), backend=backend)
    encryptor = cipher.encryptor()
    encrypted_data = encryptor.update(padded_data) + encryptor.finalize()

    recipient_infos = []
    for recipient, pad_bytes in recipients:
        with open('{}.public.pem'.format(recipient), 'rb') as fp:

            plain = b'\x00\x02' + pad_bytes + b'\x00' + key
            pub = cert.public_key.unwrap()
            encrypted_key = gmpy2.powmod(
                int.from_bytes(plain, 'big'),
                pub['public_exponent'].native,
                pub['modulus'].native)
            encrypted_key = int(encrypted_key).to_bytes(
                (pub['modulus'].native.bit_length() + 7) // 8,
                'big')

        recipient_infos.append(cms.KeyTransRecipientInfo({
            'version': 'v0',

            'key_encryption_algorithm': {

            'encrypted_key': encrypted_key,
        }))

    ci = cms.ContentInfo({

        'content': {

            'recipient_infos': recipient_infos,
            'encrypted_content_info': {

    cms_bytes = ci.dump()

`message.author` is not a string. It is a `discord.Member` object. It gets printed as your tag because `print` automatically wraps the statement in `str(..)`.

Reiterating these as these are still present in your code:
- You're using `client` for the command decorator, but you're also using `bot` in your code.
  - You are probably accidentally be using both `Client` and `Bot` when you really only want `Bot` (since it is a subclass of `Client`). If you define an instance for both, remove the `Client` and replace your decorators, etc to use `bot` instead.
- You accept a `message` object, but your decorator says you do not want anything passed. You should reinstate the decorator kwarg and use `ctx.message` to access message attributes.
- You should do `str(message.author)`, `message.author.name` or `message.author.mention` etc to get the string for your `Name:` embed value.

Additionally, judging by the whitespace around your code, I have a suspicion that this may be the only code present in your bot. Your bot cannot function with command code alone; you must instantiate a `Bot` instance and pass it a token to log into Discord with. I would recommend reading the [examples](https://github.com/Rapptz/discord.py/tree/async/examples).

You have a debugger set up. When a debugger hits a **breakpoint** it also halts all execution. That's what a debugger does. You can't 'step over' the `run` function because, like I said, it's blocking. 

Fundamentally speaking your woes with your debugger are completely irrelevant to the code you posted. Nothing will run after that `Client.run` call. **Nothing will run after that statement**. You will have no events registered. No events will be called. The only thing that will end up happening is that a KeepAliveThread is created. `asyncio` is **single-threaded**, the only thing that is running is the main thread and the keep alive thread (along with any other daemons the other modules need). 

I do not want to walk you through a debugger. I don't use PyCharm. It does not take much effort however to know that your entire debugging woes are fundamentally because of what I posted in the first comment. OK, this probably won't be a problem.  The reason `schedule.json` is not updating is because [`update_sched.py`](https://github.com/BurntSushi/nflgame/blob/db6a0b32399471aea7d36af61166ffa47ff36ea0/nflgame/update_sched.py#L21) only looks at the years 2009 through the year that [`live.current_year_and_week()`](https://github.com/BurntSushi/nflgame/blob/db6a0b32399471aea7d36af61166ffa47ff36ea0/nflgame/live.py#L107) says it currently is.  Since `live.current_year_and_week()` thinks it is still 2017 (according to [`live.CUR_SCHEDULE`](https://github.com/BurntSushi/nflgame/blob/db6a0b32399471aea7d36af61166ffa47ff36ea0/nflgame/live.py#L68)) even if you ask it to update `--year 2018` it bails out and leaves `schedule.json` alone.

If you absolutely *have* to see `nflgame` give you an updated schedule, change [this line](https://github.com/BurntSushi/nflgame/blob/db6a0b32399471aea7d36af61166ffa47ff36ea0/nflgame/update_sched.py#L21) to `range(2009, cur_year+2)` and re-run `...\nflgame\update_sched.py --year 2018`.  You'll find all of the 2018 preseason and regular season games now appear in your `schedule.json` file.

The main issue was with the original implementation of TXT record handling.

The TXT records of a domain were being presented by `dns.resolver` as a list of lists (Split TXT records). I hadn't originally realised that this was to cater for split TXT records, and so was presenting all of the split TXT records with the non-split TXT records in a single large list. This becomes problematic when a domain has TXT records which are split, as these records would be indistinguishable from other TXT records (all part of one big list).

Basically, each split record need to be concatenated into a single string for presentation as a list of TXT records, rather than a list of TXT record segments.

It wasn't specifically an issue with your patch; but your patch highlighted an existing issue in the code.

HA needs to see the presentation message from the sensor in order to add it to the UI.  Most likely, the presentation message was sent before HA started, or it was only half-received -- the ValueError message in your log output typically occurs on startup because only part of a message was received.

Make sure that your sensor code sends a presentation message on startup, and try toggling your sensor off/on after HA has started.  If the presentation message is received, you should see a message in the log file like `adding new devices: ...` with a list of sensors.

You can also enable debug mode in the HA configuration section for mysensors using `debug: True`.  This should output a debug message to the log for every message received from the serial gateway, and may help you get more information.

If persistence is enabled for HA (it is by default), then the sensor information will be saved to a pickle file so that if HA restarts, the sensors do not need to send a presentation message again.  HA does need to receive at least one presentation message to create the persistence file, though.

Hope that helps.

To get this working, I created a class that has a reference to the client something like this:
```
class ConnHandler(object):
    def __init__(self, conn, condition):
        self.conn = conn
        self.condition = condition

    def run(self):
        while self.running:
            with self.condition:
                self.condition.wait()
                if self.running:
                    self.client.stop()
                    self.client.start()
                    self.client.connect(None, None, wait=True)
                    self.client.subscribe("/queue/test", "foo", "client-individual")
```

mmh, if you use custom st-pipe you don't need st-safe-src, as your custom pipe function is supposed to provide the dataset matching a given tablestate in place of the built in logic. In your case, your custom pipe function does not provide any data: you must be doing something wrong here

In this case I think the best is not to handle the pagination (and how you load data) with smart-table but to do it on your own:
1. Don't use st-pagination

Yeah I would not want to test rendered configs using string comparison / matching. That is a complex and brittle test structure. Luckily, you might just side-step the issue of testing configs entirely.

It occured to me earlier that having `python-webpack` generate a config at all may be out of scope. Rationale as follows:

The reason I got to thinking about this is because every Webpack config I've ever seen is highly customizable. Some times you even end up writing JavaScript functions that _generate_ your webpack config based on a whole host of environment specific variables and concerns.

It seems to me like the only way to preserve the flexibility inherent in Webpack's purpose is to basically piggy-back onto `webpack.config.js` generation by allowing for value injection (from Python) but not trying to build the entire config within `python-react`.

I picture something as simple as defining `{{ py-react:KEY_NAME }}` replacements inside of a user-supplied template config file that is just run through a templating engine and then executed before being supplied to the render call. This way a user can make up any values they want/need and build a config template that matches their environment for any scenario but `python-webpack` can still use on the fly for specific components. The user can specify exactly how those components are rendered and which values need to come from `python-react` via the template key replacements and just supply them during the render call or even provide a dict of functions that computes them based on some application or state specific parameters (route name etc...).

For example, when I went to use `python-webpack` directly, it made sense to point it to a config file that I had already written in `.js` because if I wanted, I could supply a template config and dynamically inject the name of the component I wanted to render. I can't do that with `python-react` directly though. I would have to intercept it's usage of `python-webpack` or hard-code the template into `python-react` in replacement of the one that's currently used. (which I did for the time being)

The use case translates to `python-react` in a couple scenarios:

1) User has an existing `webpack.config.js` file that contains a whole bunch of `external` / `library` / `output` / `plugin` / `loader` rules that are constant for the entire project but I still want to render components on demand. I could supply a template of this config file to `python-webpack` specifying replacements in the `entry` fields for the component being rendered but the rest of the config is left untouched.

2) User has a production `webpack.config.js` separate from dev OR I have a `webpack.generate.config.js` that builds webpack config files based on certain conditions. I feed one or all of them into `python-webpack` for dynamic value injection (for components) and get consistent packing behavior across all environments without having to make `python-webpack` know anything about it.

I ended up basically replacing the config template that `python-react` uses (for now) and it was having to do that made me decide that maybe that responsibility should not live inside the `python-react` package but rather be on the user to supply and pass through to `python-webpack`.

As far as testing this route goes, you would really only need to test that value injection was occuring. You would no longer need to test permutations of templates because that's now the responsibility of the user to supply a valid config template. You only need test that the value replacement happens correctly-- simple I should think.

If I've overlooked something obvious that precludes these ideas than I apologize, I would like to spend more time familiarizing myself with all the relevant code-bases anyway.

Hi!

> Python treats them as different classes.
If Python treats them as different classes so does python-inject.

> the inject code thinks it's OK to instantiate an object (with no parameters) if there is no declared supplier of the object
Yes, it does, because it greatly simplifies the configuration. Python does not have interfaces and usually there is only one singleton implementation. You already have you graph of objects defined as @inject parameters, so no need to configure it.

> if you paid attention to the class name instead of the actual class
That’s a terrible hack. You see, from the interpreter point of view you DO HAVE two different classes. That’s the problem you need to solve, and not the one how python-inject treats their identity. Classes are references in python. Each reference identifies a unique class. Classnames mean nothing.


> On 26 Aug 2017, at 21:49, Alan Robertson <notifications@github.com> wrote:
> 
> Ran into some weirdness in using Inject. Weirdness that I consider to be a bug...
> 
> I had declared that I wanted only one copy of a certain kind of object - but it gave me two distinct objects.
> 
> It took a while to figure out the symptoms in my code were caused by that, and what the underlying cause was.
> 
> One of the consumers of these objects was in a certain module, and the other was in the same file as the object declaration.
> 
> Because I was testing this new code, I had some code below the 'if name == "main" clause.
> 
> That code thought the object class was <main.Store>, and the other consumers (and the code that set up the injection mapping) thought it was <store.Store>. Although they're the same class, Python treats them as different classes... Hence, any consumers of the object which used inject.params in main, were always going to get a unique object each time it asked for one. This happens because the inject code thinks it's OK to instantiate an object (with no parameters) if there is no declared supplier of the object. In my view, that's an error. If I declare that I need a certain kind of object in inject.params, then IMHO it's an error for me not to supply it in the injection parameters.
> 
> It would have cut several hours off the process to realize what was going on sooner - with an error message. Especially if the error message said something like: No constructor for object type blah - currently known constructors are: (list of known object types). That would have made it dead obvious what was wrong immediately.
> 
> So, I think that should be an error, at least by default (maybe all the time?).
> 
> Ideally, it would be nice to figure out how to keep this from happening, then you wouldn't have to make that an error.
> 
> Here's a thought about one approach:
> 
> Upon checking, no matter where you look, cls.name gives the same answer for both main.Store and store.store. So, if you paid attention to the class name instead of the actual class, then I believe that would solve the problem.
> 
> It seems really unlikely that someone would have two different classes of the same name, and want to supply two different constructors for them.
>

Oh, from your log the crash appears to be hours after startup, not at startup as the summary says, if that is the case, it is most likely due to a bug that we have fixed in master.  I don't recommend running master as we're in the middle of some pretty big changes, but you can workaround that bug by setting this in your `/etc/watchman.json` file, or scope it to your watch by putting it in a `.watchmanconfig` file in the root of the watch:

```
{"gc_interval_seconds":0}
```

This turns off file aging which normally runs approximately every 12-24 hours and is used to prune out long-deleted files.  This will cause memory usage to increase as you generate garbage files (eg: file names based on a random string that are short lived), but this is pretty small and you most likely won't notice it.

https://facebook.github.io/watchman/docs/config.html#gc_interval_seconds has the documentation for this option.

In response to my last note:

> I want to say the best way forward would be to change the xml schema for add_ons so that it supports the method used everywhere else and deprecates the old method but it seems to be used in other places. Let me discuss with the team and report back here.

The reason this is different is because when creating a plan or an add on, you may want to define multiple prices for multiple currencies. For transactions, invoices, or subscription addons (for example) you only have one currency. I still think we could make this more consistent but that should at least answer why it is the way it is.

As for the solution to this problem, you should specify the currency when creating an add-on or a plan. The currencies and values can be passed in as kwargs to the `Money` class:

```python
plan = recurly.Plan.get('gold')

addon = recurly.AddOn(
    add_on_code='ipaddresses',
    name='Extra IP Addresses',
    unit_amount_in_cents=recurly.Money(USD=100,EUR=500) # 1$ or 5€ per IP address
)

plan.create_add_on(addon)
```

In general, where the XML API expects a currency list type, use `Money`. When the object in question has a separate `currency` attribute, use an `int`.

So the problem, as you pointed out, is that languageClient-neovim sets `stderr=PIPE`.  What is happening is the client blocks waiting to read from stdout, Pyls fills up stderr buffer and blocks waiting to write to stderr and we end up in deadlock.

See here for more explanation:
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/1180606/using-subprocess-popen-for-process-with-large-output

What languageClient-neovim _should_ be doing is use select to read off both stdout and stderr and then throwing away stderr if it doesn't think its relevant. Alternatively it could set stderr=None to just drop it entirely, it could redirect it to appear on the client's stderr, or it could pass in a file object to redirect stderr to disk.

I agree that Pyls should have more configurable logging and will work on implementing that, but it's not in any language server contract that logs cannot be written to stderr.

Equally changing the log level isn't sufficient as in the case there is a lot of `ERROR` messages it will still hang! Just happening less frequently. 

So I think the solution here is for us to support configurable logging, and to make things easy for clients maybe a command line switch to redirect stderr to a file, but also for languageClient-neovim to properly handle stderr.

One thing to decide, regardless how we decide to return the information, is how to represent the expiry time. Apparently browsers store them in seconds (since EPOCH?) except for Safari that uses milliseconds. We should always use the same format, regardless the browser, and I don't think a plain integer (or float) is the best representation.

Probably the best idea would be using Python's standard [datetime object](https://docs.python.org/2/library/datetime.html#datetime-objects). They have useful attributes that can be accessed in data like `${expiry.year}` and they are fully supported also by Robot's own [DateTime library](http://robotframework.org/robotframework/latest/libraries/DateTime.html). Regardless the format we use, the same format should be accepted as an input when setting the expiry time (#891).

That won’t work either because it escapes single quotes, and Windows don’t want you to.

```python
import shlex
import os
import re


def escape_grouped_arguments(s):
    """Prepares a string for the shell (on Windows too!)

    Only for use on grouped arguments (passed as a string to Popen)
    """
    if s is None:
        return None

    # Additional escaping for windows paths
    if os.name == "nt":
        s = "{}".format(s.replace("\\", "\\\\"))
    return '"' + s.replace("'", "'\\''") + '"'


def my_quote(s):
    return '"{0}"'.format(re.sub(r'(\\*)"', r'\1\1\\"', s))

s = "print('hello world')"
print('SO:   ', 'python', '-c', escape_grouped_arguments(s))
print('shlex:', 'python', '-c', shlex.quote(s))
print('mine: ', 'python', '-c', my_quote(s))
```

Hi @djKooks ,

Thanks for helping us work to improve the code-base :).

I originally considered doing something like this using enums, but ultimately decided not to because I wanted users to be able to easily pass in string values (rather than having to import/dig for the appropriate value). Obviously, if we just have the string literals replaced by string variables, then strings can still be used at runtime, but then the auto-documentation strings will be a little harder to parse (e.g. `help(...presto)`). Typos are unlikely to be an issue because we check immediately whether the string is in a predefined list of drivers, and notify the user, hence it's not clear to me why having it has a variable reduces typing errors. I guess when a variable is used, you can benefit from auto-completion?

Anyway... while I don't feel strongly either way, I'm slightly biased toward maintaining the status quo, so at this stage I think I won't merge this one in :).

I wanted to let you know, that I still experienced problems with CSRF validation in combination with the `invenio-userprofiles` module.
For the RegistrationForm which did not include `invenio-userprofiles` everything worked as expected. With invenio-userprofiles enabled, form validation stopped working again. (I used the [create_csrf_disabled_registrationform()](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-oauthclient/blob/4539c272ca10a11b2e3c8ff379eb98e830bb9eed/invenio_oauthclient/utils.py#L227-L235] )-function from `invenio-oauthclient`.)

The problem was, that in the subform created by `invenio-userprofiles` CSRF-validation was still active. After deleting the `csrf_token` in the subform and also setting csrf-validation to false, the form validation succeeded.

My current solution is to disable csrf-validation as follows for `Flask-WTF>=0.14` for a given form:

```python
def disable_csrf(form):
  form.meta.csrf = False
  if 'csrf_token' in form:
    del form.csrf_token
    for f in form:
```

Yes. There are 2 valid use-cases outside of what transaction currently does (implicit abort if begin is called twice), and there isn't a right answer so I just want them to be possible.

1. If a transaction is already active then raise an error or warning.

2. If a transaction is already active then start using it instead of calling `.begin()` and avoid calling commit/abort yourself - leave it up to the caller that started the transaction.

Neither of these changes any assumptions about how transaction works, it's just a question of who gets to begin and commit. It's currently possible via `manager._txn is None` but I'd just like to see a public api for it.

```
>>> from geopy.geocoders import Nominatim
```

Actually [this is stated](http://geopy.readthedocs.io/en/1.12.0/#geopy.geocoders.Nominatim) in the docs.

2. High volume of requests (Nominatim rate limits are quite low – no more than 1 request per second). In this case you're better off switching to another geocoding service (try MapQuest or Photon as close alternatives based on OSM).

I'm not sure we should have our repr's be dependent on a global variable in another module.  There's a reason people say global variables are evil, and while I don't completely agree with that sentiment, this is the kind of reason why people have that opinion.  Behavior can change in ways that you don't control.  That's not necessarily a good feature.

That's why I didn't use the numpy repr function for things like LookupTable.  I used bits like
```
'x=array(%r), f=array(%r)'%(self.x.tolist(), self.f.tolist())
```
precisely to avoid the numpy array's repr function.

For `Image`, I understand the desire to switch to using the numpy repr to make it easy to truncate long arrays.  It's easier than coding up the truncation ourselves, and I'm ok with that.  But I'd rather not spread that to too many other classes.  I don't think having a precise repr for most of these classes is so annoying.  They aren't typically the unassigned return value of a function (like `drawImage`), so they don't show up on the screen so often in interactive mode.  Also, being 1-dimensional, even when they do show up on the screen, they don't tend to explode it the same way images are prone to do.

As for the hash stuff (and eq), I don't mind if you want to change the implementation to use the values directly rather than the repr.  However, if you do, you'll need to step up the unit tests wrt the hash function.  One advantage of having a precise repr is that a hash based on it is (basically) unique.  You don't have to worry about whether you forgot to include some relevant parameter as part of the hash function.  So if you get away from that, you should probably add tests that two objects that are the same except for one minor detail have different hash values.  It might be hard to have confidence that we check all the important combinations of this.

https://github.com/ansible/tower-cli/blob/master/tower_cli/utils/resource_decorators.py#L22

But this is different from the problem that `$(cat ~/.ssh/id_rsa)` won't really work - that's because the line breaks are removed. tower-cli could have a conditional to convert spaces into line breaks, but that kind of highly-automatic behavior can have surprising consequences.

I'm looking for feedback on ways to approach the problem of supporting swagger2 and oas3 simultaneously.

I can imagine a few approaches:
1. add a bunch of if/else statements all over the place, get it working, then refactor.
 - pros:
 -- very incremental
 -- leverage existing tests
 - cons:
 -- increases code complexity dramatically
 -- generally yucky

2. refactor the operations class into classes that deal with the different specs (resolve refs, path/global defaults), and then slim out the connexion operation class to focus on the webserver side of the operation.
 - pros:
 -- allows for a refactor first before adding oas3 support
 -- relatively incremental
 - cons:
 -- crams oas3 support into swagger2 data structures, negating a lot of the benefits of oas3

3. convert all code to oas3 style data structures, then convert swagger2 spec -> oas3 spec immediately upon loading the spec.
 - pros:
 -- reap benefits of simplified spec, new features
 -- oas3 is completely backwards compatible, so conversion should be lossless
 -- separation of concerns (spec differences are captured in the converter only)
 - cons:
 -- lose benefits of lots of existing test coverage
 -- not very incremental
 -- no existing swagger2 -> oas3 spec converter in python (that I know of)

I've put a little bit of time into options 1, and 2. I'll probably give 3 a shot next and report back.
The main pain points so far have been around requestBody vs. body/form parameters, and content mimetypes vs. consumes/produces.

Let me know what you all think! I'm currently leaning toward option 3 because it would delegate the responsibility of backwards compatibility and keep the code clean. Unfortunately that strategy isn't very incremental, so I imagine it will be tough to upstream.

2. Could you elaborate on how this might be against any W3C standard?  This changes only situations in which the Jira plugin was checking that the response had **any** content -- not any situations in which this was **using** the content returned.

To be as absolutely clear as I can: this Jira library is 100% broken for downloading any large *binary* attachments from issues unless this PR (or a similar one) is merged.  Binary attachments, as I'm sure you're aware, do not have a character encoding, but the code in question (using `.text` instead of `.content`) will cause the process to attempt to detect the content's encoding, even if one doesn't exist, and that can take a [very, very long time](https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/issues/2359).


Obviously kerberos is supported since it's being used in the browser, but for some reason it seems ``requests_kerberos`` is believing that the server does not support it. The actual area of ``requests_python`` that is deciding that kerberos is unsupported is [here](https://github.com/requests/requests-kerberos/blob/master/requests_kerberos/kerberos_.py#L285-L292). I believe it is not finding the expected negotiate value in the "www-authenticate" header. Doing a plain ``requests`` call shows this as there is no negotiate in it's "WWW-authenticate" response.

Using ``force_preemptive=True`` in ``requests_kerberos`` does send up the proper negotiate header, but the server rejects it. It's quite odd.

**tl;dr**

I think this is a server misconfiguration of some kind.

model.py

    # -*- coding: UTF-8 -*-
    import random,time,os
    from collections import Counter, namedtuple

    import numpy as np
    import dynet as dy

    from tools import initCemb, prepareData
    from test import test

    np.random.seed(970)

    Sentence = namedtuple('Sentence',['score','score_expr','LSTMState','y','prevState','wlen','golden'])

    class CWS (object):
        def __init__(self,Cemb,character_idx_map,options):
            model = dy.Model()
            self.trainer = dy.MomentumSGDTrainer(model,options['lr'],options['momentum']) # we use Momentum SGD
            self.params = self.initParams(model,Cemb,options)
            self.options = options
            self.model = model
            self.character_idx_map = character_idx_map
            self.known_words = None
        
        def load(self,filename):
            self.model.populate(filename)

        def save(self,filename):
            self.model.save(filename)

        def use_word_embed(self,known_words):
            self.known_words = known_words
            self.params['word_embed'] = self.model.add_lookup_parameters((len(known_words),self.options['word_dims']))

        def initParams(self,model,Cemb,options):
            # initialize the model parameters  
            params = dict()
            params['embed'] = model.add_lookup_parameters(Cemb.shape)
            for row_num,vec in enumerate(Cemb):
                params['embed'].init_row(row_num, vec)
            params['lstm'] = dy.LSTMBuilder(1,options['word_dims'],options['nhiddens'],model)
            
            params['reset_gate_W'] = []
            params['reset_gate_b'] = []
            params['com_W'] = []
            params['com_b'] = []

            params['word_score_U'] = model.add_parameters(options['word_dims'])
            params['predict_W'] = model.add_parameters((options['word_dims'],options['nhiddens']))
            params['predict_b'] = model.add_parameters(options['word_dims'])
            for wlen in range(1,options['max_word_len']+1):
                params['reset_gate_W'].append(model.add_parameters((wlen*options['char_dims'],wlen*options['char_dims'])))
                params['reset_gate_b'].append(model.add_parameters(wlen*options['char_dims']))
                params['com_W'].append(model.add_parameters((options['word_dims'],wlen*options['char_dims'])))
                params['com_b'].append(model.add_parameters(options['word_dims']))
            params['<BoS>'] = model.add_parameters(options['word_dims'])
            return params
        
        def renew_cg(self):
            # renew the compute graph for every single instance
            dy.renew_cg()

            param_exprs = dict()
            param_exprs['U'] = dy.parameter(self.params['word_score_U'])
            param_exprs['pW'] = dy.parameter(self.params['predict_W'])
            param_exprs['pb'] = dy.parameter(self.params['predict_b'])
            param_exprs['<bos>'] = dy.parameter(self.params['<BoS>'])

            self.param_exprs = param_exprs
        
        def word_repr(self, char_seq, cembs):
            # obtain the word representation when given its character sequence

            wlen = len(char_seq)
            if 'rgW%d'%wlen not in self.param_exprs:
                self.param_exprs['rgW%d'%wlen] = dy.parameter(self.params['reset_gate_W'][wlen-1])
                self.param_exprs['rgb%d'%wlen] = dy.parameter(self.params['reset_gate_b'][wlen-1])
                self.param_exprs['cW%d'%wlen] = dy.parameter(self.params['com_W'][wlen-1])
                self.param_exprs['cb%d'%wlen] = dy.parameter(self.params['com_b'][wlen-1])

            chars = dy.concatenate(cembs)
            reset_gate = dy.logistic(self.param_exprs['rgW%d'%wlen] * chars + self.param_exprs['rgb%d'%wlen])
            word = dy.tanh(self.param_exprs['cW%d'%wlen] * dy.cmult(reset_gate,chars) + self.param_exprs['cb%d'%wlen])
            if self.known_words is not None and tuple(char_seq) in self.known_words:
                return (word + dy.lookup(self.params['word_embed'],self.known_words[tuple(char_seq)]))/2.
            return word

        def greedy_search(self, char_seq, truth = None, mu =0.):
            init_state = self.params['lstm'].initial_state().add_input(self.param_exprs['<bos>'])
            init_y = dy.tanh(self.param_exprs['pW'] * init_state.output() + self.param_exprs['pb'])
            init_score = dy.scalarInput(0.)
            init_sentence = Sentence(score=init_score.scalar_value(),score_expr=init_score,LSTMState =init_state, y= init_y , prevState = None, wlen=None, golden=True)
            
            if truth is not None:
                cembs = [ dy.dropout(dy.lookup(self.params['embed'],char),self.options['dropout_rate']) for char in char_seq ]
            else:
                cembs = [dy.lookup(self.params['embed'],char) for char in char_seq ]

            start_agenda = init_sentence
            agenda = [start_agenda]

            for idx, _ in enumerate(char_seq,1): # from left to right, character by character
                now = None
                for wlen in range(1,min(idx,self.options['max_word_len'])+1): # generate word candidate vectors
                    # join segmentation sent + word
                    word = self.word_repr(char_seq[idx-wlen:idx], cembs[idx-wlen:idx])
                    sent = agenda[idx-wlen]

                    if truth is not None:
                        word = dy.dropout(word,self.options['dropout_rate'])
                    
                    word_score = dy.dot_product(word,self.param_exprs['U'])

                    if truth is not None:
                        golden =  sent.golden and truth[idx-1]==wlen
                        margin = dy.scalarInput(mu*wlen if truth[idx-1]!=wlen else 0.)
                        score = margin + sent.score_expr + dy.dot_product(sent.y, word) + word_score
                    else:
                        golden = False
                        score = sent.score_expr + dy.dot_product(sent.y, word) + word_score


                    good = (now is None or now.score < score.scalar_value())
                    if golden or good:
                        new_state = sent.LSTMState.add_input(word)
                        new_y = dy.tanh(self.param_exprs['pW'] * new_state.output() + self.param_exprs['pb'])
                        new_sent = Sentence(score=score.scalar_value(),score_expr=score,LSTMState=new_state,y=new_y, prevState=sent, wlen=wlen, golden=golden)
                        if good:
                            now = new_sent
                        if golden:
                            golden_sent = new_sent

                agenda.append(now)
                if truth is not None and truth[idx-1]>0 and (not now.golden):
                    return (now.score_expr - golden_sent.score_expr)

            if truth is not None:
                return (now.score_expr - golden_sent.score_expr)

            return agenda

        def forward(self, char_seq):
            self.renew_cg()
            agenda = self.greedy_search(char_seq)
            now = agenda[-1]
            ans = []
            while now.prevState is not None:
                ans.append(now.wlen)
                now = now.prevState
            return reversed(ans)


        def backward(self, char_seq, truth):
            self.renew_cg()
            loss = self.greedy_search(char_seq,truth,self.options['margin_loss_discount'])
            res = loss.scalar_value()
            loss.backward()
            return res

    def dy_train_model(
        max_epochs = 30,
        batch_size = 256,
        char_dims = 50,
        word_dims = 100,
        nhiddens = 50,
        dropout_rate = 0.2,
        margin_loss_discount = 0.2,
        max_word_len = 4,
        load_params = None,
        max_sent_len = 60,
        shuffle_data = True,
        train_file = '../data/train',
        dev_file = '../data/dev',
        lr = 0.5,
        edecay = 0.1,
        momentum = 0.5,
        pre_trained = '../w2v/char_vecs_100',
        word_proportion = 0.5
    ):
        options = locals().copy()
        print ('Model options:')
        for kk,vv in options.items():
            print ('\t',kk,'\t',vv)

        Cemb, character_idx_map = initCemb(char_dims,train_file,pre_trained)

        cws = CWS(Cemb,character_idx_map,options)

        if load_params is not None:
            cws.load(load_params)
            test(cws, dev_file, 'result')

        char_seq, _ , truth = prepareData(character_idx_map,train_file)
        
        if max_sent_len is not None:
            survived = []
            for idx,seq in enumerate(char_seq):
                if len(seq)<=max_sent_len and len(seq)>1:
                    survived.append(idx)
            char_seq =  [ char_seq[idx]  for idx in survived]
            truth = [ truth[idx] for idx in survived]
        
        if word_proportion > 0:
            word_counter = Counter()
            for chars,labels in zip(char_seq,truth):
                word_counter.update(tuple(chars[idx-label:idx]) for idx,label in enumerate(labels,1))
            known_word_count  = int(word_proportion*len(word_counter))
            known_words =  dict(word_counter.most_common()[:known_word_count])
            idx = 0
            for word in known_words:
                known_words[word] = idx
                idx+=1
            cws.use_word_embed(known_words)

        n = len(char_seq)
        print ('Total number of training instances:',n)
        
        print ('Start training model')
        start_time = time.time()
        nsamples = 0
        for eidx in range(max_epochs):
            idx_list = [i for i in range(n)]
            if shuffle_data:
                np.random.shuffle(idx_list)

            for idx in idx_list:
                loss = cws.backward(char_seq[idx],truth[idx])
                if np.isnan(loss):
                    print ('somthing went wrong, loss is nan.')
                    return
                nsamples += 1
                if nsamples % batch_size == 0:
                    cws.trainer.update()

            cws.trainer.update_epoch(1.)
            end_time = time.time()
            print ('Trained %s epoch(s) (%d samples) took %.lfs per epoch'%(eidx+1,nsamples,(end_time-start_time)/(eidx+1)))
            test(cws,dev_file,'../result/dev_result%d'%(eidx+1))
            os.system('python score.py %s %d %d'%(dev_file,eidx+1,eidx+1))

    # -*- coding: UTF-8 -*-
    from collections import defaultdict

    import numpy as np
    import random
    import gensim
    import re

    def initCemb(ndims,train_file,pre_trained,thr = 5.):
        f = open(train_file)
        train_vocab = defaultdict(float)
        for line in f.readlines():
            sent = line.split()
            for word in sent:
                for character in word:
                    train_vocab[character]+=1
        f.close()
        character_vecs = {}
        for character in train_vocab:
            if train_vocab[character]< thr:
                continue
            character_vecs[character] = np.random.uniform(-0.5/ndims,0.5/ndims,ndims)
        if pre_trained is not None:
            pre_trained = gensim.models.Word2Vec.load(pre_trained)
            pre_trained_vocab = set([ w for w in pre_trained.wv.vocab.keys()])
            for character in pre_trained_vocab:
                character_vecs[character] = pre_trained[character]
        Cemb = np.zeros(shape=(len(character_vecs)+1,ndims))
        idx = 1
        character_idx_map = dict()
        for character in character_vecs:
            Cemb[idx] = character_vecs[character]
            character_idx_map[character] = idx
            idx+=1
        return Cemb,character_idx_map

    def SMEB(lens):
        idxs = []
        for len in lens:
            for i in range(len-1):
                idxs.append(0)
            idxs.append(len)
        return idxs

    def prepareData(character_idx_map,path,test=False):
        seqs,wlenss,idxss = [],[],[]
        f = open(path)
        for line in f.readlines():
            sent = line.split()
            Left = 0
            for idx,word in enumerate(sent):
                if len(re.sub('\W','',word,flags=re.U))==0:
                    if idx >Left:
                        seqs.append(list(''.join(sent[Left:idx])))
                        wlenss.append([len(word) for word in sent[Left:idx]])
                    Left = idx+1
            if Left!=len(sent):
                seqs.append(list(''.join(sent[Left:])))
                wlenss.append([ len(word) for word in sent[Left:]])
        seqs = [[ character_idx_map[character] if character in character_idx_map else 0 for character in seq] for seq in seqs]
        f.close()
        if test:
            return seqs
        for wlens in wlenss:
            idxss.append(SMEB(wlens))
        return seqs,wlenss,idxss

    # -*- coding: UTF-8 -*-
    import re
    from tools import prepareData

    def test(cws,filename,output_path):

        def seg(char_seq,text):
            lens = cws.forward(char_seq)
            res, begin =[], 0
            for wlen in lens:
                res.append(''.join(text[begin:begin+wlen]))
                begin+=wlen
            return res

        char_seqs = prepareData(cws.character_idx_map,filename,test=True)
        fo = open(output_path,'wt')
        seq_idx = 0 
        for line in open(filename).readlines():
            sent = line.split()
            Left = 0
            output_sent = []
            for idx,word in enumerate(sent):
                if len(re.sub('\W','',word,flags=re.U))==0:
                    if idx>Left:
                        words =seg(char_seqs[seq_idx],list(''.join(sent[Left:idx])))
                        seq_idx += 1
                        output_sent.extend(words)
                    Left = idx+1
                    output_sent.append(word)
            if Left!=len(sent):
                words = seg(char_seqs[seq_idx],list(''.join(sent[Left:])))
                seq_idx += 1
                output_sent.extend(words)
            output_sent = '  '.join(output_sent)+'\r\n'
            fo.write(output_sent)
        fo.close()

This is WAI (or WADNAI if you prefer -- Working as Designed, not as intended). `timestamp_value` is stored as a a fixed-point number in Cloud Datastore. You can see this in the [sort order specification](https://cloud.google.com/datastore/docs/concepts/entities#properties_and_value_types), Integers and Date/times are listed together in the sort order.

In the indexes, the property value is stored without additional information, in this case the indication that this is a datetime. In Cloud Datastore, projection queries aren't just field masks -- they indicate to the system that it should only perform an index scan and not do the full entity lookup. This means it won't have the opportunity to lookup the additional information.

We hope to be able to fix this eventually, but it has a large impact on backwards compatibility.

The test case for the read rows retry that is not working is test_yield_retry_rows.

It is using a _MockRetryableIterator, which should read a row successfully in order to get last_scanned_row_key, then throw an exception for next(). This should trigger retry to run the code again and then next() should return the second row successfully.

What is happening instead is that the exception is NOT being handled by Retry. This seems to be related to the fact that the exception is being thrown when yield is being used. It is peculiar to yield only, since it was tried without yield and the exception was handled properly in the Retry.

In order to reach 100% code coverage, the test case needs Retry to catch the exception and do retry EVEN when yield is used. 

Any suggestions on why yield behaves differently and what can be done to get this to work are welcome.

One possible resolution is that I: (1) close this ticket as it doesn't seem to be a `dill` problem… and (2) open a new ticket to explore the potential to inject the `dill` `Unpickler` behavior into the one used by `pickle` -- or to remove custom behavior from the `Unpickler` in `dill`.  Note also that this behavior could however, potentially be resolved in `dill`, as the example above shows.  The test does not fail when `dill.settings['recurse'] = True`.  So an alternative to (2) above is to figure out what exactly is the issue when `'recurse' is `False`, and see if there's a workaround.

So… my inclination is to close this ticket saying "don't use `pickle` to unpickle something from `dill`, but if you do, then make sure you have set `'recurse'` to `True` -- and I will also open a new ticket with regard to modifying/injecting the `Unpickler` behavior, or the behavior from `'recurse'=True`. No; and you couldn't even if you wanted to. You *will* however need to make a new branch in your forked repository, maybe called `tdd` for simplicity's sake. > Create scenario
> Run behave --stop
> Add code which makes the the scenario work
> Add unittests which tests all the different pieces of code that make the acceptance test / scenario pass
> Commit this to the branch on the py-docx remote

Close. something like this:

* Commit docs (analysis page)
* Commit scenario(s) you want to start out with. All tests should pass (both acceptance and unit) using `behave --tags=-wip`. One of the new acceptance tests should fail using `behave --stop`. This is the first functionality unit to unit test and add.
* Devise unit test for indicated functionality. E.g. if failure is `Document has no attribute 'bookmarks'` you add a unit test `it_provides_access_to_its_bookmarks()`. When the test is written and failing, you write the code for `Document.bookmarks`. When that passes it's time for a commit.
* The acceptance tests should all still pass with the `-wip` flag and the acceptance test should fail in a *new* place without it. All unit tests must pass on *every* commit; the wip bit is just for acceptance tests.
* Repeat until all the acceptance tests pass without the `-wip` flag (then remove that decorator from passing acceptance tests.
* Then you're either done with the feature or it's time for the next acceptance test(s).
* I'll review commits as you say they're ready, and either merge them or provide feedback on how they need to be fixed. Generally by this point things start to move pretty quickly.

> Good one, the w:id's are the keys. In that case, word does trigger a repair error when duplicates are found.

This is a good experimental finding. Let's make sure this appears in the Word Behaviors section of the analysis document (so we don't have to re-run the experiment when we forget :)

> I currently count the occurences of <w:bookmarkStart> and based on that supply the new bookmark with a id.

This won't do. If you have ids 2, 3, and 4, by this algorithm you'd assign 4. You need to either assign `max+1` (5 in this case) or filter through and use the lowest unused value. The `max+1` approach is simpler and I think should work just fine. There is a finite risk of overflow that the "reuse numbering gaps" approach addresses, but I doubt anyone would ever encounter it. I think the maximum ID number is up around 4 billion.

```
def _add_number_range(run, name):
    """ add a number range field to a run
    """
    fldChar = OxmlElement('w:fldChar')  # creates a new element
    fldChar.set(qn('w:fldCharType'), 'begin')  # sets attribute on element
    instrText = OxmlElement('w:instrText')
    instrText.set(qn('xml:space'), 'preserve')  # sets attribute on element
    instrText.text = 'SEQ %s \* ARABIC' % name
    
    fldChar2 = OxmlElement('w:fldChar')
    fldChar2.set(qn('w:fldCharType'), 'separate')
    fldChar3 = OxmlElement('w:t')
    fldChar3.text = "Right-click to update field."
    fldChar2.append(fldChar3)
    
    fldChar4 = OxmlElement('w:fldChar')
    fldChar4.set(qn('w:fldCharType'), 'end')
    
    r_element = run._r
    r_element.append(fldChar)
    r_element.append(instrText)
    r_element.append(fldChar2)
    r_element.append(fldChar4)
```

Hi @jsma and @tweekmonster.

We're looking at the test failures, the project just recently came out of beta however it became apparent that pyxb was not optimal for API response processing as it is really intended for strict schema validation and that obviously hampers backward compatibility.   We have switched to objectify for response handling and we have some tests flux for sure, never a good thing.  You have probably now realized why it seems a little "java-y", it's because we generate the classes from our XSD and hence the programming model is more or less the same across all languages.  Definitely we lose some language efficiencies and style but we felt we made up for that by having very low maintenance SDKs.
RE Python 3 support, great feedback, we're going to revisit our strategy there.

Finally, REST APIs are coming and we'll be providing a new Python SDK at that time.  We'll still try to drive maintenance efficiencies by using swagger to generate the SDK but all the trials we've done so far indicate this will yield a much better outcome than XML/XSD.

We're going to review and merge the PRs, thanks for those and look out for further updates.

Brian

Sorry for that I just went through it carefully. Some preliminary notes for database adapter:

1. `Transaction` and `Blob`:
	* Instead of transactions, the recent design is storing **blobs** as just some binary data.
	*  `CollationHeader` includes `blobs_root` instead of `transaction_root`, copy from @vbuterin: the `blobs_root` is a simple Patricia tree root, where the keys are two bytes: `0x0000`, `0x0001`, `0x0002`, etc; a collation can contain a maximum of 65536 blobs.

I have seen similar error messages on other recipes (unrelated to this issue) and found that it helped to add a `track_features` directive to the package in question. That is, `msvc_runtime/meta.yaml` should contain something like:
```yaml
package:
  name: msvc_runtime
  version: 1.0.0

build:
  features:
    - vc9
  track_features:
    - vc9
  ...
```
(likewise for the other variants of that package). To test if this helps, it might be possible to edit `<conda-root>\pkgs\msvc_runtime-1.0.0-vc9_0\info\index.json` etc. and add the line:
```json
  "track_features": "vc9", 
```
(hoping that conda won't overwrite the change with the package's original content). If this indeed resolves the issue, upstream must fix their recipes and create new packages.

Moreover, I don't quite understand why all `msvc_runtime` variants have version numbers `1.0.0` or `1.0.1`. It would be much more logical to match the package version to the compiler version, i.e. `msvc_runtime-9.0` for vc9. Then one could also enforce a particular msvc version by calling `conda install msvc_runtime=9.0`. The present design that distinuishes variants only by build string may also relate this issue to the version resolution bug described in https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/1589.

I found the reason. It is because non-English OS message is not encoded utf-8 (in my case, CP949)

On installation, ``subprocess``_call function is called:
https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/33303158f070c0eda1ce0bbcb7807ddc7a4972d6/conda/gateways/subprocess.py#L62

This function calls ``ensure_text_type`` to validate standard output:
https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/7005e366a2a7dcd19430114c4a3d30f2eb735995/conda/common/compat.py#L154

This function raises an Unicode error because subprocess' standard output include not-encoded-in-utf-8 string.

@vermaete @joshrsmith @suoto Let's see if we can work out the user stories and verify that those are met.
* We want to get `__doc__` and `__version__` from the `vunit` package and to make that work when you have several versions installed/cloned it should be part of the `vunit` package you have imported and not something you get from `pip` or similar.
* For maintenance reasons I want single source. The exception is `README.rst` which has to exist to get the basic information presented in the Github [repo](https://github.com/VUnit/vunit). I added a [test case](https://github.com/VUnit/vunit/blob/master/vunit/test/lint/test_readme.py) which verifies that  `README.rst` == `__doc__` to avoid mistakes.
* Then we have the case of installing/cloning VUnit. It has to work regardless if you have an existing VUnit version or if it is the first time. Installing with `pip` without any existing VUnit on the path works for  me. The 

``` python
from vunit.about import version, doc
```
 statement in `setup.py` is handled so I assume that its directory is on the path used when running `pip`. It also works if I run `setup.py` directly but only as long as I'm running from that directory. That's a bit of a weak spot. Maybe I should read and call `exec` with the `about.py` code directly rather than importing it.
* Another issue is that if you're cloning VUnit `__version__` shouldn't return `0.66.0` as it is doing today. The repo has changed after that release. I think the best way of working, if we want to be strict, is to change the version directly after a new release has been uploaded to PyPI. So if 0.67.0 is uploaded the version should be immediately changed to 0.68.0-rc.1. Anyone checking versions automatically should be able to handle these formats. The rules about this are defined by http://semver.org/

@kraigher The Jenkins test result certainly looks nicer with the proposed change but since it affects many users I'd like a second opinion. I will contact two VUnit + Jenkins users and see what they think.

The answer is just to employ another filter.  You can exclude a `pattern` `filtertype` with a `value` of `'%Y.%m.%d'`:

```
    - filtertype: pattern
      kind: timestring
      value: '%Y.%m.%d'
      exclude: True
```

This filter should work anywhere in the chain, but it might be easier to visualize if it appears _before_ the age filter with the weekly timestring.

I strongly feel that our developer docs should recommend installing
everything using Conda by default. Having people go and grab python and all
the low level stuff themselves will results in them simply getting
frustrated and doing nothing. Also, if the developers *start* as users,
they will already have Jupyter/IPython installed using conda in most cases,
so recommending they do something different will send them down the rabbit
hole of having two installs.

Probably the best approach is to point them to our regular user install
docs, and then starting the dev docs based on the assumption that they
already have a working user install. This is important because we do have
to show them how to do things like uninstall the non-dev version in
different ways (pip, conda, apt-get).

I wouldn't really equate `PolyType` and `ModelType` in this way.

`PolyType` is special in that it is passed a further list of types. The arguments must be instances because that's how they get their properties.

All the other types, including `ModelType`, are "regular" types that define a concrete format for a field. Their arguments generally do not require instantiation: the class `Band` is a meaningful parameter to a `ModelType`, just like the integer `30` would be a meaningful parameter to `StringType(30)`. Instantiating the `Band` doesn't add any relevant information to it.

Default data would be passed as a `default` argument to the `ModelType`, not the `Band`. This is already possible today.

That said, it would be easy to modify `ModelType` so that it would accept both classes and their instances, but I don't readily see what this would accomplish.

In this case, I think you're right. Our git calls are respecting your projects `.gitattributes` when they shouldn't be. We override `$HOME` to disable your global `.gitconfig`, so hopefully there's a similar hack we can add for this file. Awesome bug find. Let me know if you have a git project I can clone to repro exactly what you're seeing.

(A little clarification for future reference. One of tricky things about peru's internals is that there are two very different ways we use git. One is the totally-internal-implementation-detail of moving file trees around and dumping them in your project, the *tree cache*. The other is the kind-of-exposed-to-users role of cloning remote git repos, the *git plugin*. Global configuration is only suppressed for the tree cache, because the git plugin might need things like your SSH keys and proxy settings.)

It doesn't, unfortunately.  

Suppose we have `max_in_flight == 2` and 4 initial connections.  

1. We initialize the `RDY` counts by calling `_send_rdy` with `value == 1` for each connection.  

2. The first such call appropriately sends `RDY == 1` and only afterwards attempts to update the reader's `total_rdy` from its initial value of 0 to 1:
```
self.total_rdy = max(self.total_rdy - conn.rdy + value, 0)
```
But at this point `self.total_rdy == conn.rdy == 1`.  Thus we return from `_send_rdy()` with `total_rdy == 0`.

3. The same happens on each subsequent call to `_send_rdy()`.  Each time, the condition you pointed to:
```
(self.total_rdy + value) > self.max_in_flight
```
is `(0 + 1) > 2`.  Control continues to `conn.send_rdy(value)`.  Thus each of the 4 connections is initialized to `RDY == 1`.

Here's a demonstration.

Run 4 local `nsqd` instances:
```
$ ps -ocommand | grep nsqd | grep -v grep
nsqd -lookupd-tcp-address=127.0.0.1:4160 -node-id 0 -tcp-address 0.0.0.0:9000 -http-address 0.0.0.0:9001 -data-path=/tmp/data0
nsqd -lookupd-tcp-address=127.0.0.1:4160 -node-id 1 -tcp-address 0.0.0.0:9002 -http-address 0.0.0.0:9003 -data-path=/tmp/data1
nsqd -lookupd-tcp-address=127.0.0.1:4160 -node-id 2 -tcp-address 0.0.0.0:9004 -http-address 0.0.0.0:9005 -data-path=/tmp/data2
nsqd -lookupd-tcp-address=127.0.0.1:4160 -node-id 3 -tcp-address 0.0.0.0:9006 -http-address 0.0.0.0:9007 -data-path=/tmp/data3
```

Send a couple of msgs to each instance:
```
$ for i in {0..7}; do \
  echo foo | to_nsq \
       -nsqd-tcp-address localhost:$((9000 + 2*($i%4))) \
       -topic foo-topic \
       -rate 100 2>/dev/null; \
done
```

Anyway, you should use the `pytest_addoption` hook:
```python
def pytest_addoption(parser):
    parser.addoption(
        "--driver", action="store", help="Select a Driver"
    )
```
Then you should call it in your fixture:
```python
@pytest.fixture(scope='session')
def driver_class(request):
    driver = request.config.getoption('--driver')
    if driver is None:
        raise pytest.UsageError('--driver must be specified')
    return SUPPORTED_DRIVERS[driver]
```
I believe you can use the `required` keyword, though:
```python
def pytest_addoption(parser):
    parser.addoption(
        "--driver", action="store", help="Select a Driver", required=True
    )
```
And that will fail if `--driver` isn't passed.

So here's the thing. When I said overriding the event loop would be the way to go, I actually tested it out first using the following code:

```
import asyncio

import pytest


class CustomSelectorLoop(asyncio.SelectorEventLoop):
    """A subclass with no overrides, just to test for presence."""
    pass


@pytest.fixture()
def event_loop(request):
    """Create an instance of the default event loop for each test case."""
    policy = asyncio.get_event_loop_policy()

    policy.get_event_loop().close()

    loop = CustomSelectorLoop()
    policy.set_event_loop(loop)

    request.addfinalizer(loop.close)
    return loop
```
as you can see, the `event_loop` fixture is a little more complex to write. Your fix might solve the problem for you, but chances are third-party libraries might want to get the current event loop using asyncio anyway, and then they'll get another loop.

What I propose we do instead is move the logic of setting the produced event loop outside of the `event_loop` fixture and into pytest-asyncio itself.

```
@pytest.mark.tryfirst
def pytest_pyfunc_call(pyfuncitem):
    """
    Run asyncio marked test functions in an event loop instead of a normal
    function call.
    """
    for marker_name, fixture_name in _markers_2_fixtures.items():
        if marker_name in pyfuncitem.keywords:
            event_loop = pyfuncitem.funcargs[fixture_name]

            policy = asyncio.get_event_loop_policy()
            policy.get_event_loop().close()
            policy.set_event_loop(event_loop)

            funcargs = pyfuncitem.funcargs
            testargs = {arg: funcargs[arg]
                        for arg in pyfuncitem._fixtureinfo.argnames}
            event_loop.run_until_complete(
                asyncio.async(pyfuncitem.obj(**testargs)))

            event_loop.close()
            return True
```

To use *Letsencrypt* certs with *lighttpd*, it's necessary to provide two files and to list them in ```lighttpd.conf```. The ```ssl.pemfile``` is a combination of ```privkey.pem``` and ```cert.pem``` and the ```ssl.ca-file``` is ```chain.pem```. To create the first and copy them both into place for *lighttpd*, do:

    cat privkey.pem cert.pem > www.example.com.pem
    cp www.example.com.pem chain.pem /etc/lighttpd

Then edit the config file (described below), restart *Lighttpd* and all will be well, apart from one other problem revealed by the fine people at https://www.ssllabs.com/ssltest/

By default, *Lighttpd* uses a weak Diffie Hellman setting. This can be corrected by running the following command. It warns that it will take a long time, and it does take a long time:

    openssl dhparam -out /etc/lighttpd/dhparams.pem 2048

Assuming you have worked with the file names as described above, you'll need an SSL stanza in ```lighttpd.conf``` that looks like this:

    $SERVER["socket"] == ":443" {
        ssl.engine  = "enable"
        ssl.use-sslv2 = "disable"
        ssl.use-sslv3 = "disable"
        ssl.ca-file = "/etc/lighttpd/chain.pem"
        ssl.pemfile = "/etc/lighttpd/www.example.com.pem"
        ssl.dh-file = "/etc/lighttpd/dhparams.pem"
        ssl.cipher-list = "aRSA+HIGH !3DES +kEDH +kRSA !kSRP !kPSK"
        ssl.honor-cipher-order = "enable"
    }

Obviously, all the commands here need to be run as root or with sudo.

@rnpridgeon I think you've got it right. Firstly thanks for pointing out the pause() and resume() methods. I've started working on this when they were not present and I've been too focused on the initial picture to notice they could be of use to me.

I'll elaborate on how my problem came to be: Prior to the pause()+resume() api I could not use a consumer to subscribe to let's say 4 topics, but consume from one of them until it reaches EOF and then move on to the next topic in order of priority. So as a workaround I made 4 individual consumers that I could poll() for messages on my discretion. This bloated up the consumption logic of the workers, so to isolate these functionalities I removed it from them and made it its own process. The workflow from `[initial producers]->[workers]` became `[inital producers]->[priority routers]->[workers]`.

With newly opened eyes, I can see that I can actually return to the original workflow by having the worker's consumer subscribe to the topics and pause() on all lower priority ones. After EOF is reached I resume() the next in line. If I get a message from a higher priority topic I will pause the active lower ones. With this setup the single conumer is always polled and my original question is no longer relevant.

I guess I need to keep track of the TopicPartitions I pause(). I also need to pause() or resume() topics based on on_assign and on_revoke callbacks.

Since I've got a very promising workaround, I'm closing my question.

>     ImportError: dlopen(/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.10/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/lib-dynload/_io.so, 2): Symbol not found: __PyErr_ReplaceException

    libpython2.7.so.1.0 => /usr/local/lib/libpython2.7.so (0xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx)

The problem lies in the current implementation of the  `render` method in [powerline-lemonbar.py](https://github.com/powerline/powerline/blob/develop/powerline/bindings/lemonbar/powerline-lemonbar.py#L32): Despite the `lemonbar` process having died (thus the `BrokenPipeError`), a new Timer is created which will call `render` again after `--interval` seconds (which defaults to 1 second), producing another `BrokenPipeError`.

Some first comments:
* Distinguishing ArrayDataSource, CallableDataSource and IteratorDataSource seems very reasonable.
* The interface seems good as well, although I guess we should support just `shuffle=True` for users who don't care. We could still have `shuffle` accept a RNG to minimize the number of keyword arguments. And instead of `circular` I'd have a slight preference for `forever` or `epochs=-1` (where the latter would allow specifying the number of repetitions you want). `circular` sounds like it would repeat the same permutation over and over. And finally, for some purposes it'd be good to return the remainder batch (the last `len(datapoints) % batchsize` items ) instead of dropping it, there should be an option for that.
* I'm a bit puzzled about `CompositeDataSource`. It makes sense to have it so that you can combine multiple callables or arrays, but I'm not sure about the shuffling behaviour pulling different items from the different sources, instead of the same items. If you want that, `itertools.izip` on the generators would be the more obvious option. (And in your case, where both sources are the same, you could just double the  batchsize and feed the two halves to the two training functions.) In short, I would expect `CompositeDataSource(ArrayDataSource([x]), ArrayDataSource([y]))` to be equivalent to `ArrayDataSource([x, y])`.
* `with_params`: Hmm, this raises some additional questions. What if you pass `circular=False` to the outer `batch_iterator` call? Will this overwrite what you gave to `with_params`? I find it a dangerous path to mix the dataset representations (`DataSource`) with the iterators over them. If we want to combine iterators, we should just use `itertools.izip`, or instantiate the iterators before the loop and call `next()` on them whenever we need something. Combining datasets (as in `CompositeDataSource`) should not influence iteration.

> I think it might be a good idea to replace this with a `sample_limit` parameter

Hmm, why?

> An additional class to add data augmentation into the pipeline. I think that this would make sense.

I'm unsure how much of this belongs into Lasagne. I had long been planning to tidy up my data iteration module and release it (as a separate project). It would be good to have something in Lasagne to reduce the boilerplate needed to run an experiment, because not every user plans to write their own framework, but it cannot be as full-fledged as a library dedicated to data iteration. It should just provide the means to either train from simple array-based sources or plug in whatever more complex data iteration code you have.

I checked and it seems like libpcap 1.7.4 does indeed have the `pcap_file` function.

From the looks of the output of the setup.py it is finding the `pcap.h` header inside of `include/pcap.h` as opposed to `include/pcap/pcap.h`.
The former header is only a wrapper around the inclusion of the actual `pcap.h` header located in `pcap/pcap.h`, which means that when we look inside of it for the `pcap_file` function we don't find it and end up not defining `HAVE_PCAP_FILE`.

How are you running the setup.py script? In theory it shouldn't be looking inside of `/usr/local/Cellar` unless you have it inside of your sys.prefix.

If we want to support looking for the headers in there too some reworking of the `setup.py` is needed to give priority to the header in `pcap/pcap.h`.
